Member-only story

--

Difficulty: Hard
Category: Graph Theory & BFS
Original Problem
Problem Summary
Rob’s fence has N wooden stakes numbered 1 through N, initially with colors 1 through N respectively (stake i has color i). There are K fenceposts that divide the fence into K segments. Each second, a stake may change its color to match an orthogonal neighbor’s color (left or right neighbor on the fence).
We want to find the minimum time until all stakes between each pair of consecutive fenceposts are the same color. If it’s impossible, output -1.
Example:

• N = 5, fenceposts at positions {2, 4}

• Segments: [1], [2–3], [4–5]

• Initial: [1] [2, 3] [4, 5]

• Each segment needs uniformity
Key Challenge: Determine which color “wins” in each segment (spreads to all stakes in that segment) and how long it takes.
Understanding the Problem
What Defines a Segment?
If fenceposts are at positions p₁, p₂, …, p_K, then segments are:

• [1, p₁)

• [p₁, p₂)

• …
